burnoutLog.entry: im freaking out because i feel like im in a situation where I am so far away from anesthesia that i sound stupid for even considering it. i cant even think about reapplying in my 2nd or 3rd yer of residency let alone my first year. i just feel very lost and anicous. just moved to san francsico
burnoutLog.response:
Javier, that feeling of being far away — like the goal itself has become embarrassing to say out loud — that's one of the harder things to carry. It's not weakness. It's what happens when your ambition outpaces your current circumstances, and the gap feels humiliating instead of just... temporary.

You're a PGY-1. You literally just arrived. In a new city. After a match that didn't go the way you planned. That's a lot of disorientation stacked on top of grief. The anxiety makes complete sense.

Here's what I want to name honestly: **you're not stupid for wanting this.** You're just standing at the very beginning of a long road and trying to see the end from here — which nobody can do, and which is making you spiral.

You don't have to have reapplication figured out today. What you *do* need right now is to get your footing in San Francisco, in your intern year, in your own skin again.

**One question to sit with before next session:**

When you imagine telling someone you're still pursuing anesthesia — what specifically feels embarrassing? The goal itself, or what you think they'll think of you?

That distinction matters. We'll come back to it.

burnoutLog.entry: I am having a tough time right now and feel like i got cheated out of anestehsia. i feel like IM isnt really what i hoped it was. I just wanted to learn medicine, but it sounds like most of what i am doing is learning workflows to be a good hospitalist. I dont want that. and 2nd and 3rd year is worse with the crazy amount of clinic we have and i feel like clinic is a waste of my time. I really need help and feel pretty hurt. i just want to be in the OR all day. i hate this and feel like in my life, i never got what i deserved. starting in college, i should have gotten into UCLA , need two years off to get into med school, and then now i feel like i am going to be two years off from getting into anesthesia (since i will be donig 3 years of IM and hopefully match into an advaced/reserved spot for anesthesia), i will be 34 by the time i finish with this. I should have been 32. very sad and annoyed
burnoutLog.response:
Javier, that weight you're carrying is real and heavy. The "two years off" pattern - college, med school, now anesthesia - I can hear how that accumulates into something that feels like the universe keeps moving the goalposts on you specifically. That's exhausting and genuinely unfair-feeling. You're not being dramatic.

And you're grieving two things at once: the path you didn't get, AND being trapped in a training that feels misaligned with who you are. Watching clinic hours pile up when you want to be in an OR - that's not a small irritation. That's daily friction against your identity as a physician.

The "I never got what I deserved" thread concerns me gently - not because it's wrong to feel it, but because that story, if it hardens, can quietly poison how you show up in 2027. Bitterness is a reasonable response to repeated disappointment. It becomes dangerous when it becomes a fixed lens.

You mentioned worrying about bitterness leaking into relationships - is that happening yet, or is this more internal right now?

burnoutLog.entry: its not happening yet, but i am too ashamed to even tell people that i want to do anesthesia. like i just tell people crit care is my goal, and i wouldnt mind doing crit care/anestehsia, but i dont know. I just feel like my dream is slipping from my hands
burnoutLog.response:
That shame piece - that's important. You're carrying this dream essentially alone, hiding it behind a "safe" answer that protects you from judgment or pity if things don't work out again. That's an incredibly isolating way to move through residency.

And "my dream is slipping from my hands" - that imagery is painful. You can see it, you know what you want, but you can't quite grip it yet. That's not the same as losing it. But I won't pretend the distance doesn't hurt.

The shame worries me though. When we hide our real goals, we also cut ourselves off from support, mentorship, and accountability. The "crit care" cover story protects you emotionally but might also be quietly reinforcing the belief that anesthesia is already lost - like you're pre-grieving a failure that hasn't happened.

You have roughly 2-3 years to build a genuinely competitive anesthesia reapplication. That's real time. But shame tends to make people shrink rather than strategize.

One honest question: is there even one person in your life right now - attending, co-resident, friend, anyone - who knows the OR is your actual dream?

messages.content:
Mood today: 2/5 (Struggling).

I am having a tough time right now and feel like i got cheated out of anestehsia. i feel like IM isnt really what i hoped it was. I just wanted to learn medicine, but it sounds like most of what i am doing is learning workflows to be a good hospitalist. I dont want that. and 2nd and 3rd year is worse with the crazy amount of clinic we have and i feel like clinic is a waste of my time. I really need help and feel pretty hurt. i just want to be in the OR all day. i hate this and feel like in my life, i never got what i deserved. starting in college, i should have gotten into UCLA , need two years off to get into med school, and then now i feel like i am going to be two years off from getting into anesthesia (since i will be donig 3 years of IM and hopefully match into an advaced/reserved spot for anesthesia), i will be 34 by the time i finish with this. I should have been 32. very sad and annoyed

Javier, that weight you're carrying is real and heavy. The "two years off" pattern - college, med school, now anesthesia - I can hear how that accumulates into something that feels like the universe keeps moving the goalposts on you specifically. That's exhausting and genuinely unfair-feeling. You're not being dramatic.

And you're grieving two things at once: the path you didn't get, AND being trapped in a training that feels misaligned with who you are. Watching clinic hours pile up when you want to be in an OR - that's not a small irritation. That's daily friction against your identity as a physician.

The "I never got what I deserved" thread concerns me gently - not because it's wrong to feel it, but because that story, if it hardens, can quietly poison how you show up in 2027. Bitterness is a reasonable response to repeated disappointment. It becomes dangerous when it becomes a fixed lens.

You mentioned worrying about bitterness leaking into relationships - is that happening yet, or is this more internal right now?

its not happening yet, but i am too ashamed to even tell people that i want to do anesthesia. like i just tell people crit care is my goal, and i wouldnt mind doing crit care/anestehsia, but i dont know. I just feel like my dream is slipping from my hands

That shame piece - that's important. You're carrying this dream essentially alone, hiding it behind a "safe" answer that protects you from judgment or pity if things don't work out again. That's an incredibly isolating way to move through residency.

And "my dream is slipping from my hands" - that imagery is painful. You can see it, you know what you want, but you can't quite grip it yet. That's not the same as losing it. But I won't pretend the distance doesn't hurt.

The shame worries me though. When we hide our real goals, we also cut ourselves off from support, mentorship, and accountability. The "crit care" cover story protects you emotionally but might also be quietly reinforcing the belief that anesthesia is already lost - like you're pre-grieving a failure that hasn't happened.

You have roughly 2-3 years to build a genuinely competitive anesthesia reapplication. That's real time. But shame tends to make people shrink rather than strategize.

One honest question: is there even one person in your life right now - attending, co-resident, friend, anyone - who knows the OR is your actual dream?

just my family/gf, but no one in kaiser or SF. they sympathize with me, but obviously cant do anything about it. i wish i can just tell the PD or someone and then be able to at least shadow in the OR or have some reassurance, but right now it feels like such a far awar dream

The people who love you can hold your heart, but they can't open OR doors. That gap - emotional support without professional traction - leaves you feeling stuck even when you're surrounded by people who care. That makes sense.

And I notice what you said: "I wish I could just tell the PD." That's not a passive wish - that's you identifying exactly what you actually need. Visibility. An ally inside the system. Someone who can help make anesthesia feel real and reachable rather than a secret you're quietly protecting.

Here's what I want to gently name: keeping this hidden at Kaiser isn't just emotionally isolating - it's practically limiting. PDs talk to each other. Anesthesia faculty notice interested residents. OR exposure during IM isn't impossible, but someone has to advocate for you, and right now no one at Kaiser even knows to.

The fear of telling them is real - judgment, pity, being seen as "not committed to IM." I'm not dismissing that risk.

But you're already paying a high cost for the silence: isolation, shame, and a dream that feels like it's fading instead of building.

What specifically feels most dangerous about your PD knowing your real goals?
